# COMP110 Data Analysis Project

<!-- This is a comment. Below, you'll see code for inserting an image. To make this image appear, update <custom-path>. To add an image, save it inside the imgs folder of this repository. -->
<img src="/data-analysis/static/imgs/logo.png" alt="Image of Comp110 rainbow logo." width="500"/>

## Summary

My analysis was based on the goal of finding out whether or not class pacing affected overall understanding of course topics. I compared the "pace" and "understanding" columns from the course survey. 

## Visualizations

Graph 1: Scatter Plot
<img src="/data-analysis/static/imgs/Dot.png" alt="Scatter plot showing pace and understanding." width="500"/>

Graph 2: Line Plot
<img src="/data-analysis/static/imgs/Line.png" alt="Line plot showing pace and understanding." width="500"/>

Graph 3: Distribution Plot
<img src="/data-analysis/static/imgs/Bar.png" alt="Distribution plot of understanding scores." width="500"/>

## Conclusion

The data suggests that there may be a relationship between cource pacing and understanding, however, there is variability and no clear positive or negative correlation. Because of this variability, it would not be realistic to make the conclusion that pacing by itself is the sole factor of understanding, while there is clearly a pattern. 

In order to get a better evaluation, more data could be gathered on pacing, such as having students enrolled in the course rank which factor had the most effect on their understanding. 

One potential downside/tradeoff to adjusting pacing of the course would be that slowing down the course could decrease how much material gets covered, and impact students who prefer a faster pace. In order to test the significance of this, an expiremental approach would be temporarily adjusting pacing of topics and surveying students for feedback of their understanding.
